coredata.dc:title: High-energy short-wave blue light conversion films via carbon quantum dots for preventing retinal photochemical damage
coredata.dc:creator: Guo, Huazhang
Zhang, Xuan
Chen, Zeyu
Zhang, Liang
Wang, Liang
Xu, Jing
Wu, Minghong
coredata.dc:description: It is urgent to develop anti-high-energy short-wave blue light films for preventing retinal damage. Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) with robust optical tuning properties are potential candidates for addressing the issue. Here, bright yellow CQDs (Y-CQDs) with quantum yields up to 71% are synthesized via the alkali-catalyzed molecular fusion strategy, which demonstrates excellent anti-short-wave blue light performance. To construct anti-short-wave blue light WLEDs, the flexible transparent Y-CQD/PVA films are manufactured by mixing Y-CQDs and PVA, whose short-wave blue light conversion performance is further verified by being applied on R-, G-, B-LEDs, and WLED. Furthermore, a series of biological experiments regarding retinal protection from photochemical damage is further conducted, verifying that the Y-CQD/PVA films (≥20 wt%) have a significant protective effect on the retina. Our discoveries endow CQDs with a permit into the field of light conversion and will propel the progress of carbon-based optical materials for manufacturing forward-looking anti-short-wave blue light films and devices.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon quantum dot
Molecular fusion strategy
Anti-Short-wave blue light films
Conversion
Retinal damage assessment

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: absattachment articleinfo articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pglast pii piinorm pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure e-component body acknowledge affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast footnotes grantnumber grantsponsor grantsponsorid orcid primabst ref specialabst

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of flexible anti-blue light Y-CQD/PVA films
Anti-blue light LEDs applications via Y-CQD/PVA films
Retinal damage evolution of the Y-CQD/PVA films
Synthesis and application of anti-short-wave blue light Y-CQD/PVA films
Advanced application of human eye protection

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) The synthesis of Y-CQDs using 1-amino-2-naphthol-4-sulfonic acid as a precursor under adjusting different parameters. (b) TEM image and corresponding lateral size distribution of Y-CQDs. (c) High-resolution TEM image of Y-CQDs. (d) AFM image and corresponding height profile of Y-CQDs. (e) FTIR spectrum of Y-CQDs. (f) High-resolution N1s spectrum of the Y-CQDs. (g) High-resolution S2p spectrum of the Y-CQDs. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

(a) PL (red) and PLE (green) spectrum of the Y-CQDs. (b) The 3D spectrum of Y-CQDs. (c) The quantum yield of Y-CQDs. (d) Transmittance spectra of Y-CQDs at the beginning and after 120 days (inset: the photograph of Y-CQDs under daylight). (e) The long-term stability tests of Y-CQDs. (f) The temperature stability tests of Y-CQDs. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

(a) Photographs of the PVA film and Y-CQD/PVA films prepared using different contents of Y-CQDs (1, 5, 10, 20, 30, and 40 wt%) under daylight. (b) SEM cross-section images of the PVA film and Y-CQD/PVA films were prepared using different contents of Y-CQDs (1, 5, 10, 20, 30, and 40 wt%). (c) The PL spectra of PVA and Y-CQD/PVA films on the B-LED chip were prepared using different contents of Y-CQDs (1, 5, 10, 20, 30, and 40 wt%). (d) Photographs of blue/green/red LED chips coved without and with Y-CQD/PVA films. (e) The PL spectra of the anti-short-wave blue light Y-CQD/PVA film on G- and R-LED chips. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

(a) Diagrammatic sketch of retinal photochemical damage induced by blue light. (b) Representative ERG scotopic 3.0 responses (group I, II, III, and IV). (c) Statistical analysis of ERG scotopic 3.0 a-wave amplitudes. (d) Statistical analysis of ERG scotopic 3.0 b-wave amplitudes. (Statistical analysis was performed using SPSS software, a one-way analysis of variance followed by a Bonferroni's/Tukey post hoc test was used to determine significance. P < 0.05 was considered significant. Values shown in c and d are presented as mean values ± S.D.; n = 6, *P < 0.05, **P < 0.005, ***P < 0.0005 and ****P < 0.00005 compared between each measurement stage.) (e) Representative OCT findings of the retina around the optic disk (group I, II, III, and IV). (f) Representative fundus photography images (group I, II, III, and IV). (g) TEM photomicrographs of representative inner segments, outer segments and outer nuclear layer sections (group I, II, III, and IV). (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

head.ce:title: High-energy short-wave blue light conversion films via carbon quantum dots for preventing retinal photochemical damage

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: It is urgent to develop anti-high-energy short-wave blue light films for preventing retinal damage. Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) with robust optical tuning properties are potential candidates for addressing the issue. Here, bright yellow CQDs (Y-CQDs) with quantum yields up to 71% are synthesized via the alkali-catalyzed molecular fusion strategy, which demonstrates excellent anti-short-wave blue light performance. To construct anti-short-wave blue light WLEDs, the flexible transparent Y-CQD/PVA films are manufactured by mixing Y-CQDs and PVA, whose short-wave blue light conversion performance is further verified by being applied on R-, G-, B-LEDs, and WLED. Furthermore, a series of biological experiments regarding retinal protection from photochemical damage is further conducted, verifying that the Y-CQD/PVA films (≥20 wt%) have a significant protective effect on the retina. Our discoveries endow CQDs with a permit into the field of light conversion and will propel the progress of carbon-based optical materials for manufacturing forward-looking anti-short-wave blue light films and devices.

ce:section.ce:para: Optoelectronic devices have attracted huge attention owing to their immense potential applications in multiply fields, especially lighting and display [1–8]. White light-emitting diodes (WLED) stand out from numerous lighting devices because of their inherent highlights of compact volume, high energy efficiency, and long lifetime [9–17]. However, like the great majority of lighting devices, the emitted light from current WLED remains to contain high-energy short-wave blue light (400–480 nm), which leads to the damage of the human retina under continuous irradiation [18–22]. Despite the appearance of commercial anti-short-wave blue light films that have tried to address the malfunction, the current films encounter some challenges in practical applications: (1) the films ineffectively block short-wave blue light; (2) the chromatic aberration after using the films in applications is serious; (3) the long-term stability of the films need further improvement. Urgently, excessive consumption of electronic products is a common phenomenon in the current information era, which exacerbates the retinal damage of human eyes by short-wave blue light. Therefore, it is essential to design and synthesize emerging anti-short-wave blue light films to fulfill the above demands.

Nanoscale carbon quantum dots (CQDs) are ideal candidates for manufacturing films to eliminate short-wave blue light due to its environmentally friendly, flexible designability, low cost, and large-scale preparation [23–30]. Furthermore, tunable and long-term optical properties endow the CQDs with a robust anti-light performance in special wavelengths. Most importantly, the super-small size of CQDs (less than 5 nm) is a benefit for hindering its agglomeration during the preparation and application process of the films. Although the previously reported work opened the prelude of the CQD-based short-wave blue light blocking films [31], the challenge of inferior anti-short-wave blue light effect and unknown relevant biological verification still hinder the potential applications of CQD-based anti-short-wave blue light films.

Herein, we synthesized high quantum yields (QYs, up to 71%) of yellow fluorescence CQDs (Y-CQDs) via a bottom-up alkali-catalyzed molecular fusion strategy. With optimized reaction parameters, the obtained Y-CQDs exhibited a narrow-dispersion size of 2.3 nm, strong anti-short-wave blue light behavior, and long-term durability over 120 days. High-efficiency anti-short-wave blue light films were established by mixing polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) and Y-CQDs with different mass ratios, then the film's robust short-wave blue light conversion capability was clarified by application on R-, G-, B-LEDs, and WLED. To verify the ability of Y-CQD/PVA films in preventing retinal damage, relevant biological experiments were carried out and gave a positive conclusion that the Y-CQD/PVA films can selectively filter short-wave blue light for protecting the retina from photochemical damage. Our findings pave a new choice to replace the original anti-short-wave blue light films for really slowing down retinal damage. Furthermore, it also provides material support for investigating the next-generation display and lighting devices with anti-short-wave blue light in the future.

The 1-amino-2-naphthol-4-sulfonic acid, 4-amino-1-naphthalenesulfonic acid sodium, 1-amino-2-naphthol hydrochloride, PVA (viscosity: 27.0–34.0 mPa s, the degree of alcoholics is 98–99), ethanol and ethylenediamine were purchased from Adams (Shanghai Titan Scientific Co., Ltd., China). 0.5% tropicamide phenylephrine and 0.4% oxybuprocaine hydrochloride were purchased from Santen Pharmaceutical Co. Ltd. (Osaka, Japan). 1.7% sodium hyaluronate was purchased from Bausch & Lomb (Shandong, China), 2% sodium pentobarbital, 4% paraformaldehyde, 2.5% glutaraldehyde, 0.1 M phosphate buffer, 1% osmium tetroxide, 3% uranyl acetate, lead nitrate, eponate 12, 1% toluidine blue, hematoxylin and eosin were purchased from Gefan Biotechnology Co. Ltd (Shanghai, China). Blue LEDs (460 ± 10 nm, 1500 lux) were purchased from Xuzhou Ai Jia Electronic Technology Co. Ltd. (HCB-SKDS-1000; Xuzhou, China). All chemical reagents were used without further purification.

Briefly, 1-amino-2-naphthol-4-sulfonic acid (0.010 g) was dissolved in ethanol (10 mL) and EDA (0.4 mL) was adding the above solution. Then the mixture was transferred to a poly(tetrafluoroethylene) (Teflon)-lined autoclave (25 mL) and heated at 180 °C for 12 h. After cooling down the room temperature, the preliminary product solution was filtered through a 0.22 μm microporous membrane and purified by dialysis (MWCO 3500) for several days. Last, the Y-CQDs powder was obtained for further characterization or application. The control experiment of CQDs was synthesized under the same conditions for Y-CQDs choosing 1-amino-2-naphthol hydrochloride and 1-naphthylamine-4-sulfonic acid as the precursors.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of flexible anti-blue light Y-CQD/PVA films
ce:section.ce:para: PVA (10 g) was dissolved in 200 mL DI water at 90 °C. When the solution cooled down at room temperature, the final colorless and transparent solution (0.05 g/mL) was obtained. Then, the different contents (0, 1, 5, 10, 20, 30, and 40 wt%) of Y-CQDs aqueous solution were mixed with PVA solution under stirring for 0.5 h. Subsequently, the homogeneous solutions were transferred in a round petri dish (d = 60 mm) or a square petri dish (240 × 240 mm) and dried at 35 °C for 2 days.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Anti-blue light LEDs applications via Y-CQD/PVA films
ce:section.ce:para: The Y-CQD/PVA films with different Y-CQDs contents as interrupting blue light films were applied with B-, G-, R-LED, and WLED chips for filtering blue light and other lights. Shortly, the film covered the chips directly and ensured no light leakage from the chips. Then, the optical properties of the film-covered LEDs were measured with HAAS-2000 and the CIE color coordinates were marked with CIE1931xy.v1.5 software.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Retinal damage evolution of the Y-CQD/PVA films
ce:section.ce:para: Five-week-old female SD rats were provided by Hunan SJA Laboratory Animal Co. Ltd. (Hunan, China) and were maintained under a 12 h:12 h light-dark cycle. The procedures used were approved by the Institutional Animal Care and Use Committee of Central South University. The SD rats were randomly distributed into four groups using the random number table method: group I consisted of rats kept in darkness and served as a blank control; rats in groups II, III, and IV were exposed to blue LED illumination covered Y-CQD/PVA films with set at a different mass ratio of 40 wt%, 20 wt%, and 0 wt%, respectively. The Y-CQD/PVA films were fixed in front of the light sources, then placed above the top of the cages. Rats were dark-adapted for 24 h, pupils were dilated with 0.5% tropicamide phenylephrine 30 min before exposure, then continuously exposed to the blue light for 5 h. Altering light exposure, rats were kept in darkness for 24 h. After 5 days of continuous light, all rats underwent ERG, fundus photography, OCT H&E staining, and TEM observation. The control group of rats went through all the experimental steps in the darkness.

Retinal electrical responses were detected using electroretinography (Roland RETI port/scan 21, Ganzfeld Q450, Germany). Retinal fundus images and OCT scans were recorded with a Micron IV Retinal Imaging Microscope and OCT Scan Head equipped with a rat objective lens (Phoenix Research Laboratories, Pleasanton, CA, USA). Electroretinography (ERG) (Roland RETI port/scan 21; Ganzfeld Q450, Germany) was performed 5 days after light exposure to detect retinal electrical responses. Rats were dark-adapted overnight and prepared under dim red illumination. The rats were anesthetized intraperitoneally with 20 mg/kg body weight of 2% sodium pentobarbital, pupils were dilated with 0.5% Tropicamide Phenylephrine (Santen Pharmaceutical Co. Ltd, Osaka, Japan), corneas were anesthetized with 0.4% oxybuprocaine hydrochloride (Santen Pharmaceutical Co. Ltd, Osaka, Japan). Active electrodes were placed on the cornea, the reference electrodes were placed subcutaneously in the anterior scalp behind the eyes, and the ground electrodes were placed subcutaneously in the tail.

Fundus photography and optical coherence tomography (OCT) were recorded during the same session 5 days after light exposure with a Micron IV Retinal Imaging Microscope and OCT Scan Head equipped with a rat objective lens (Phoenix Research Laboratories, Pleasanton, CA, USA). The rats were anesthetized and pupils were dilated as described above, eyes were lubricated with 1.7% sodium hyaluronate (Bausch & Lomb, Shandong, China). The OCT system featured a broadband super luminescent diode of 830 nm for a light source customized for retinal imaging of rats. The scan region on the rat retina was 1.8 mm in the X and the Y direction. Linear or circle OCT scans consisted of a series of 1024 single-point A-Scans. Recordings were made at 20 positions for each eye using the StreamPix 6 and Micron OCT commercial software (Phoenix Research Labs). Scale bars were set at 100 μm. Average retinal thickness and the thickness of the outer nuclear layer (ONL)thickness were assessed at the optic nerve and outer retina using the Insight-Animal OCT Segmentation Software (Phoenix Research Labs) which can automatically detect and measure each retinal layer.

Hematoxylin and Eosin (H&E) stained retinal morphology were acquired by using a light microscope (Olympus, Japan, DX53). H&E staining was performed 5 days after light exposure. The SD rats were killed with a lethal intraperitoneal injection of sodium pentobarbital. The left eyes were immediately enucleated and fixed in 4% paraformaldehyde for 24 h at 4 °C. The eyes were embedded in paraffin and were cut into sections of 5 μm thickness through the optic nerve, prepared in the standard way, then stained with hematoxylin and eosin (H&E). Retinal morphology was observed, and photomicrographs were taken using a light microscope. The thickness of the outer nuclear layer (ONL) was quantified at 240 μm intervals from the optic nerve head to the ora Serrata using the Image J image processing program. The results from three randomly selected sections averaged for each eye.

Ultrastructural retinal morphology was observed with a transmission electron microscope (Hitachi HT7700, Japan). TEM was performed 5 days after light exposure. The SD rats were killed with a lethal intraperitoneal injection of sodium pentobarbital. The right eyes were immediately enucleated and fixed in 2.5% glutaraldehyde in 0.1 M phosphate buffer for 24 h at 4 °C. The upper temporal retina was dissected and post-fixed in 1% osmium tetroxide for 2 h. Following serial dehydration, the tissue was stained with 3% uranyl acetate and lead nitrate, then embedded in Eponate 12. Sections of 1 μm thickness were stained with 1% toluidine blue and examined with a light microscope. Sections of 0.1 μm thickness were analyzed with a transmission electron microscope.

TEM observations were measured by a Japan Hitachi HT7700. AFM images were performed using a Dimension ICON (Bruker, Santa Barbara, US). SEM images were characterized by Phenom Pharos (Thermo Fisher, Netherlands). FTIR spectrum was characterized with FTIR absorption spectrometer (TGA8000, PerkinElmer, USA). XRD spectra were carried out by a Rigaku (SmartLab-II, Japan). Raman spectra were measured using Cora 5001 (Anton Paar OptoTec GmbH, Lise-Meitner-Str., Germany) at 532 nm as an excitation light source. XPS spectra were obtained with a Thermo ESCALAB 250Xi spectrometer. PL spectra were characterized using a Hitachi 7000 spectrophotometer and UV–vis absorption spectra were measured on a U-3010 spectrophotometer. The time-resolved PL spectra were collected by Horiba FluoroMax Plus. The transmittance performances of the films were clarified by Horiba Duetta. The PL QYs of CQDs solutions with different colors were obtained by comparing the integrated PL intensity and absorption value using different references. The blue and yellow fluorescent CQDs selected quinine sulfate (QYs: 58%, in 0.1 M H2SO4) and rhodamine 6G (QYs: 95%, in H2O) as the reference, respectively. The optical properties of the WLED were measured with HAAS-2000 and the CIE color coordinates were marked with CIE1931xy.v1.5 software. All the photographs of CQDs and CQD/PVA films were photographed with a camera (D7000, Nikon). Retinal electrical responses were detected using electroretinography (Roland RETI port/scan 21, Ganzfeld Q450, Germany). Retinal fundus images and OCT scans were recorded with a Micron IV Retinal Imaging Microscope and OCT Scan Head equipped with a rat objective lens (Phoenix Research Laboratories, Pleasanton, CA, USA). H&E stained retinal morphology was acquired by using a light microscope (Olympus, Japan, DX53). Ultrastructural retinal morphology was observed with a transmission electron microscope (Hitachi HT7700, Japan).

The bright fluorescence Y-CQDs was synthesized via a one-pot molecular fusion strategy using 1-amino-2-naphthol-4-sulfonic acid and ethylenediamine (EDA) as precursors in Fig. 1 a. The 1-amino-2-naphthol-4-sulfonic acid supplies the structural framework of CQDs during the molecular fusion process due to its low carbonization temperature, and the EDA introduced nitrogen into the CQDs’ structure can passivate the surface of CQDs with amino groups to improve the photoluminescence (PL) QYs. By the regulation of the reaction conditions (concentration of precursor, EDA concentration, temperature, and reaction times), the optimum QYs of 71% of the Y-CQDs can be achieved (Fig. S1 and Table S1). The prepared Y-CQDs exhibited a uniform and mono-dispersing with an average diameter of about 2.3 nm from transmission electron microscopy (TEM) (Fig. 1b). The high crystallinity structure of Y-CQDs is revealed in the high-resolution TEM image, and the interlayer spacing of 0.21 nm corresponds to the graphite (100) plane (Fig. 1c) [32]. The topographic height of the Y-CQDs is 2.1 nm, as shown in the atomic force microscopy (AFM) image (Fig. 1d), elucidating that the Y-CQDs primarily consist of ∼six layers of graphite. A broad (002) peak centered at around 23.16° in the X-ray powder diffraction (XRD) pattern of Y-CQDs (Fig. S2a) showed that some functional groups on the surface of Y-CQDs enlarge their layer spacing. Two peaks at 1386 cm−1 and 1535 cm−1 exhibited in the Raman spectrum of Y-CQDs (Fig. S2b) corresponding to the disorder D band and graphited G band. The intensity ratio I G/I D of 1.44 indicates the high degree graphitic structure of Y-CQDs, which is consistent with the result of high-resolution TEM. The chemical structure of Y-CQDs was represented by Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectrum and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). As shown in Fig. 1e, the stretching vibrations of the amino (NH2) and hydroxy (OH) groups are located at 3242 to 3382 cm−1. Besides, CO/CN at 1657 cm−1, sp2 CC at 1591 cm−1, C–N at 1531 cm−1, C–O at 1344 cm−1, sulfonic (SO3) at 1036 cm−1, and C–S at 1126 cm−1 were distinctly observed in the FTIR spectrum of Y-CQDs. These abundant peaks evidence that the Y-CQDs are co-functionalized by multiple functional groups (e.g., OH, NH2, COOH, and SO3). The XPS spectra were carried out to further investigate the surface electronic structure of Y-CQDs. The full XPS spectrum (Fig. S2c and Table S2) displays four peaks at 285.29 (62.77 at%), 399.87 (11.11 at%), 532.31 (22.68 at%), and 169.05 (3.44 at%) eV for C1s, N1s, O1s, and S2p, respectively. According to the high-resolution C1s XPS spectrum (Fig. S2d), the C1s band of Y-CQDs can be divided into four peaks, corresponding to C–C/CC (284.80 eV), C–O/C–N (285.42 eV), C–S (286.36 eV) and CO (288.85 eV), respectively. In the N1s spectrum (Fig. 1f), three different peaks at 398.67, 399.52 and 400.74 eV can be recognized, attributing to pyridine N, nitrile N, and pyrrolic N, respectively. The O1s spectrum of Y-CQDs contains three peaks of C–O (531.77 eV), CO (533.05 eV), and OS (536.49 eV) bands, respectively (Fig. S2e). Besides, there are two peaks located at 168.44 and 169.47 eV in the S2p spectrum (Fig. 1g), relating to C–S and SO3, respectively [9,33–36]. The XPS data further verified the FTIR analysis results to unravel the edge-structure building blocks of Y-CQDs.

The optical properties of the optimum Y-CQDs were further investigated, as shown in Fig. 2 a. The UV–vis absorption spectrum of Y-CQDs exhibits two strong peaks centered at 343 nm and 360 nm, which were assigned to the π→π* transition of the aromatic CC bond and n→π* transitions of the CO/CN bonds, respectively [37,38]. Meanwhile, there is an obvious strong excitonic absorption band peaked at 425 nm and the max absorption edge can expand to 520 nm, which covers the whole region of high-energy short-wave blue light. A fluorescent emission peak of 535 nm can be observed for Y-CQDs at the excitation wavelength of 470 nm. Moreover, the 3D fluorescence spectrum of the Y-CQDs (Fig. 2b) demonstrates an excitation-independent behavior, which is verified by the PL spectra under different excitation wavelengths (Fig. S3a) and the monoexponential lifetimes of 10 ns in Fig. S3b. Choosing the rhodamine 6G (QYs: 95%) as a reference, the optimal QY for the Y-CQDs is 71%, which evidences similar fluorescent performance with rhodamine 6G (Fig. 2c). Remarkably, the transmittance spectrum of Y-CQDs exhibits the filtration effect within the ∼480 nm region with no aggregation in the solution for 120 days, unraveling the long-term anti-short-wave blue light properties of Y-CQDs (Fig. 2d).

Notably, the Y-CQDs retain long-term stability for 120 days of storage (Fig. 2e and Fig. S3c) and temperature durability (Fig. 2f and Fig. S3d), which is good for the long-term usage of the CQD-based optoelectronics devices. To gain the possibility in the multiply applications, the solvation effect of Y-CQDs was investigated by transferring the Y-CQDs to different solvents (Figs. S3e–k). When the polarity of the choice solvent is larger than ethanol, the Y-CQDs in these solvents remain to emit bright yellow fluorescent. In contrast, the Y-CQDs in the solvent of weaker polarity follow the order of blueshift fluorescent. Importantly, the Y-CQDs dispersed in different solutions also exhibit excitation-independent behaviors, combining with ultralong storage and thermal stability, the Y-CQDs is expected to be applied to WLED as a high-performance phosphor.

To gain insight into the relationship between the structure of precursors and fluorescent properties, three control experiments were designed in Fig. S4 and Table S3. When the EDA does not participate in the reaction, the product emits blue light under the UV irradiation and the QYs is low to 8% (Fig. S4a), which demonstrates that EDA is responsible for redshifting the fluorescence and increasing the QYs of CQDs [39]. As shown in Fig. S4b, the SO3 group of 1-amino-2-naphthol-4-sulfonic acid plays a role to enhance the QYs of the synthesized CQDs. Meanwhile, the blue fluorescent CQDs (QYs = 19.5%) was achieved by using the precursor without the OH group under the same reaction conditions, which inferences that the OH group may be the chromophore and further improve the OYs of the product (Fig. S4c). From the above results, we can conclude that the EDA and OH group play an important role to modulate the color of CQDs whereas all the functional groups of the precursor can strengthen the QYs of CQDs. The discoveries will guide the design of novel high-efficiency CQDs by choosing the suitable precursors of a special structure.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis and application of anti-short-wave blue light Y-CQD/PVA films
ce:section.ce:para: Inspired by the above optical data of the Y-CQDs that can filter the short-wave blue light, the CQD-based films contributed to resolving the issue in the WLED application. The Y-CQD/PVA films with different contents of Y-CQDs (0, 1, 5, 10, 20, 30, and 40 wt%) were prepared and displayed in Fig. 3 a. It is noted that the color of the Y-CQD/PVA films changes from colorless to yellow with increasing Y-CQD contents. Meanwhile, the Y-CQD/PVA films also exhibit their superior flatness when the area increases to 400 cm2 (Fig. S5). All the Y-CQD/PVA films exhibit a uniform thickness, which is about 142–145 μm (Fig. 3b). The PL spectra of the Y-CQD/PVA films clarified their anti-blue light capacity (Fig. 3c and Fig. S6). The blue LED chip was used as the excitation source, and the normalized intensity of the short-wave blue light by using PVA film was unchanged. Identically, when covering the Y-CQD/PVA films (≤5 wt%) on the blue LED chip, the normalized intensity of the short-wave blue light is slightly weakened. In opposite, the normalized intensity of blue light remarkably reduced with the increasing additional contents of Y-CQDs from 5 wt% to 20 wt%. Meanwhile, when the content of Y-CQDs exceeds 30 wt%, the short-wave blue light converts to the long-wavelength lights. So, Y-CQD/PVA film not only acts as an anti-blue light film but also plays the role of a conversion film under the excitation of the blue LED. Generally, the full-color display in electronic products is composed of red (R), green (G), and blue (B) LEDs (Fig. 3d and e). We used three R-, G-, and B-LEDs to illustrate the anti-short-wave blue light reality effects of Y-CQD/PVA films. The performance of filtering short-wave blue light of the R/G-LEDs and B-LED are opposite. It is worth noting that when the R/G-LEDs were employed with Y-CQD/PVA film, the green and red light almost nonchanged, revealing the Y-CQD/PVA film has high selectivity to cut off harmful short-wave blue light and is unimpactful to other light in the visible region.

To verify the applicability of Y-CQD/PVA films for general lighting, the Y-CQD/PVA films were applied in the commercial WLED to confirm their excellent anti-short-wave blue light performance (Figs. S7 and S8). The normalized intensity of the short-wave blue light in the PL spectra by using PVA film was unchanged. There is an obvious weakening phenomenon of the short-wave blue light intensity until the content of Y-CQD/PVA film increases to 20 wt%. Simultaneously, when the WLED were covered by the Y-CQD/PVA films (≥30 wt%), the intensity of blue light was remarkably reduced. However, the luminous color of WLED gradually turns yellow with the increasing content of covered Y-CQDs/PVA films. Meanwhile, The CIE chromaticity coordinates of WLED were shifted to the yellow area with the increasing concentration of Y-CQDs in covered Y-CQDs/PVA films (Fig. S8), which further illustrates the light conversion function of the synthesized Y-CQDs/PVA films. To prove the anti-blue light effects of Y-CQD/PVA films via various thicknesses, the films of various thicknesses by adding the different volumes of 30 wt% Y-CQDs/PVA solution (6, 8, 10, and 12 mL) were analyzed (Figs. S9 and S10). Their corresponding SEM cross-section thickness is 80, 118, 144, and 160 μm, respectively. The anti-high-energy short-wave blue light effect of Y-CQD/PVA films (≥144 μm) is slightly better than these films (<144 μm). Although the Y-CQD/PVA film with more thickness (160 μm) has a better anti-blue light effect, the color of the covered WLED is dark yellow, which is not conducive to the promotion of commercial applications. So, we chose the thickness of 144 μm of Y-CQD/PVA films. Based on the above results, the Y-CQD/PVA film is a robust candidate for eliminating the harmful short-wave blue light in WLED and other display scenes.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Advanced application of human eye protection
ce:section.ce:para: To evaluate the retinal protective effect of Y-CQD/PVA films, the blue LED-induced retinal photochemical damage of rats was established and displayed in Fig. 4 a and Fig. S11 [40–42]. Sprague-Dawley rats were randomly divided into four groups as described in methods, then the blue LEDs (460 ± 10 nm, 1500 lux) were covered with a PVA film and Y-CQD/PVA films of different contents respectively (Fig. S12). Representative electroretinography (ERG) response curves of rats in each group were revealed in Fig. 4b and c. Predictably, compared with group I (control), the a-wave and b-wave amplitudes gradually decreased in group III (20 wt% Y-CQD/PVA film) and group IV (PVA film), whereas no significant difference was found in group II (40 wt% Y-CQD/PVA film). Meanwhile, the b-wave implicit time increased progressively in group III and group IV compared to group I, while no significant difference was found in group II (Fig. 4d and Figs. S13–S14). These results indicate that blue light-induced retinal function impairment is attenuated by adding Y-CQD/PVA films and the protective effect of films improved with the increasing mass ratio of CQDs.

The retinal morphologies of rats were further observed via optical coherence tomography (OCT) scans and retinal fundus images, as shown in Fig. 4e and f. In comparison to group I, optic disk border ambiguity with narrowing of retinal arteries, obvious structure disarrangements, and a severe decrease in the outer nuclear layer (ONL) thickness were noticed in group IV. Meanwhile, the basic retinal structure of group III appeared to be preserved, and its ONL thickness was between groups I and IV. Differing from groups III and IV, the retinal structure of rats in group II exhibited no obvious abnormalities, which was consistent with the ERG results.

The protective effect of anti-short-wave blue light Y-CQD/PVA films on retinal morphology at the cellular level was further verified by TEM and hematoxylin and eosin (H&E) staining. As shown in Fig. 4g and Fig. S15, photoreceptors were damaged to varying degrees in group II to IV compared with the intact structures and regular arrangements of the photoreceptors in group I. Disruptions of the outer and inner segments (OSs and ISs) including vacuolated outer discs, swelling, dissolved mitochondria, and nucleolar damage containing karyolysis and pyknosis along with a decrease of the ONL thickness were observed in groups III and IV. Photoreceptor damage was pronounced in group IV, exhibiting obvious and severe structure disarrangement and nuclei deformation, while in group III, OSs and ISs roughly retained normal basic structure, but showed increased gaps, mitochondria swelling and dissolving was mild, and morphologies of most of the nuclei were normal. However, group II showed intact retinal structure and regular arrangement of the photoreceptors, showing only slightly increased gaps of the Oss and ISs. Overall, our results advantageously confirm the capability of anti-short-wave blue light of Y-CQD/PVA films to prevent retinal photochemical damage and may provide a new choice to replace the original filter films for really reducing retinal damage.

Although the full types of the high-CRI WLED have been accomplished by the reported CQDs, these high-performance WLEDs remain to encounter the shortcoming of harmful short-wave blue light, which causes retinal damage of human eyes. Summarily, we designed a function-oriented Y-CQDs for addressing this disadvantage. The QY of Y-CQDs can be promoted to 71% by regulating their synthesis factors. A series of comparison experiments on precursors verifies that the EDA and OH groups are beneficial to modulating the emitting color of Y-CQDs, meanwhile all the functional groups of the precursor also can strengthen the QY of Y-CQDs. The uniform dispersion of size (small to 2.3 nm), thin thicknesses (2.1 nm), and abundant functional groups of Y-CQDs also play agglomeration-inhibited roles in their films. In addition, the Y-CQDs exhibited the filtration effect within the ∼480 nm region and retained long-term stability for 120 days of storage and temperature durability, which is good for the long-term consumption of the CQD-based optoelectronic devices. Naturally, the flexible ultra-thin Y-CQD/PVA films were fabricated by allocating the Y-CQDs and PVA with the appropriate mass ratio. The 30 wt% Y-CQD/PVA films not only exhibited highly efficient short-wave blue light conversion properties but also allowed other light in the visible region access smoothly. Eventually, our feasibility of Y-CQD/PVA films used in actual WLED can effectively block the harmful short-wave blue light. The bio-experiments on protection from photochemical damage to the retina of rats further verified the effect of Y-CQD/PVA films in eliminating short-wave blue light. ERG responses demonstrated a marked reduction in retinal functional damages of rats by exploiting anti-short-wave blue light Y-CQD/PVA films. Fundus photography images, OCT scans, H&E staining, and TEM results further confirmed their outstanding protective effect for attenuating retinal morphological damage in rats. Therefore, it is noted that the level of shielding of bio-experimental results can effectively provide protection from high-energy short-wave blue light hazards.

Our discoveries in this work constitute a valuable proof of concept of the synthesized carbon-based materials for developing anti-short-wave blue light practical films. Our findings realize high-energy short-wave blue light conversion and may cause considerable repercussions to generate brand new envisages of carbon-based materials for blocking hazardous UV light or short-wave blue light. We will attempt more systems that can apply our designed materials, such as sunglasses, sun umbrellas, sun protection clothing, and even sunscreen in the near future.

Huazhang Guo: Writing – original draft, Investigation. Xuan Zhang: Writing – original draft, Investigation. Zeyu Chen: Visualization, Investigation. Liang Zhang: Visualization, Investigation. Liang Wang: Supervision, Writing – review & editing, Investigation, Funding acquisition. Jing Xu: Supervision, Investigation. Minghong Wu: Supervision, Investigation, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: §H. G. and X. Z. contributed equally to this work. This project was sponsored by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Nos. 21901154, 21671129), Shanghai Pujiang Program (21PJD022), the Program for Changjiang Scholars and Innovative Research Team in University (No. IRT17R71).

The following is the Supplementary data to this article:

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.carbon.2022.08.003.
